text: Teach For America (TFA) has become a gigantic force in national discussions of teacher preparation and has received hundreds of millions of dollars in public and private funds. College students with no formal training as educators are sent to low-income schools, rural and urban, for a two-year stint as teachers. Corps members of TFA have proliferated by about 2,000% since the program was begun in 1990. Views on the impact of the “TFA Teacher” are highly contested. To some, the program provides an unprecedented opportunity to recruit the best and brightest graduates as teachers, if only for a couple of years. To others, the TFA program has distracted us from real reforms that would truly benefit the education of children in our nation’s schools and constitutes a bad experiment in the lives of poor children who deserve access to knowledgeable, skilled, and dedicated and stable teachers. While non–peer-reviewed reports commissioned by the corporation that funds TFA and other advocates of the program have periodically surfaced to claim that TFA graduates have a highly positive impact on student learning, the few peer-reviewed articles that have examined the matter paint a mixed picture. Whether the impact is positive, negative, or minimal appears to depend on the particular set of conditions included in the question of whether TFA teachers on balance improve the educational attainments of poor children. We make a number of recommendations designed to help the nation’s largest teacher preparation program address its goal of helping to improve the nation’s schools and to ensure that the funds it receives from philanthropy and the government yield high returns to all children in our nation who deserve a better education. Our recommendations are for the Board of TFA, for federal and state educational authorities, and for school districts across the nation.
